speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll three with Morris Venezia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll start again. And you were talking about what happened in this hospital after you through the grenades and you helped take out the wounded. What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened then was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the German start again the same way like they did in Thessaloniki. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was after the Italian capitulated. And everybody in Athens-- even some people, they got away from Thessaloniki and went in Athens. They were hiding. Of course, was the rich people that had the gold coins with them and tipping here and there to be able to reach Athens. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] The last week [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were talking with Dario Gabbai and my brother to go on Friday to give their last presents-- presents. Like, many other peoples in Athens, Jewish people, they were going to the synagogue and to give the present at that time, the last Friday. And after that, to go to tell the organization to send us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the partisans. It happened at that time in that last Friday, they closed up the synagogue. And we were in there all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only the-- the people who went over there to give the present. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in an hour, maybe, after they close up the streets over there, the German, all the guards of the Germans, they put two guards in front of the closed doors of the synagogue, in and out. They brought us-- they came all from the school, all the families. They came to-- to the synagogue. They put us on the-- on the cars, they had with the families [INAUDIBLE]. And they took us to the prison of Haidari [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we went to Haidari over there, it was, was our luck the last Friday with Dario. Because was no way the Germans to take us. Because we knew about Thessaloniki. So they took us to Haidari right away. They put us in the lot, an empty lot. [INAUDIBLE] Haidari in this kind of jail. And they told us to take off all the clothes. We were everybody in line to take off all the clothes to put them down. And anything in gold we had and so on, just to put them right on the floor with our clothes. With Dario and my brother, we knew what was going on. They wanted to take off, to take the-- the money we had with us, all the gold. In the meantime, my mother gave me five gold coins in case and five gold coins to my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, in case you are going to need it some time, who knows? And this morning, they didn't want to give it to us, my mother. Because was-- all those coins coming from her brothers. Because her brothers gave to my mother all the gold he had to keep it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother didn't want to spend not even a penny. Because she said, my brother's going to come out from the concentration camp. And I'm going to give it to them. Because nobody could believe it that soon as we go to the concentration camp in Poland, they're going to kill us. Nobody could believe it. And nobody knew it. Everybody knew that we're going to a labor-- a hard labor to work over there with the family and so on. So, but at the end, she understood. She gave me those five coins, gold coins, and five-- calling-- liras, liras.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we went over there, the Germans told us to leave everything down. And we were naked. About 200 people were over there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only men. The women, I don't know what happened to them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had a new suit. My brother had a new suit. Because my mother told us maybe you're going to need it. Who knows where are we going? So I had a little knife with me. And I-- what do they call them a-- Oh, you slit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tear up-- I tear up my new clothes not to give to them. I had a watch. My brother, too. And we broke the watches like this. And now, what we going to do with the five coin we got? It's 10 coins, 10 gold coins between us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not to give it to them, we gave only two to them. So I gave two to Dario Gabbai, two to his brother, two for myself, and two for my brother. And what we did with those gold coins?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know where to hide it. Was nowhere to hide it. We swallow it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I swallow two gold coins, the same thing the rest of us. And this way, after two, three days, in the bathroom we got the coins back. Because we think this is gold. It cannot harm us, except if the coins goes go right in the intestine flat. So we got the coins out. Well, that was the idea. Because all around the jail was Italian soldiers. The Germans took those Italian soldiers for guards over there, even if the Italian was capitulate. And because we knew the Italian, we said maybe we're going to have a chance to talk with one of those guards to give them the gold coins and to let us free, which it can happen. From there, they took us to communes and we took bus to the Athens trains station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long were you in Haidari before they took you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three, four days, I guess, not too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food and water while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, no, no, naturally, we-- not a chance for those things. What food? What water? Pshht. Are you kidding? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Food was no-- for them it was no-- they didn't have this intention to would give us food [INAUDIBLE]. We were-- we were animals, not people, not human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time for the gold coins, was, I remember, was one guy. He had about 25 coins, gold coins from Ioannina, was not Thessaloniki. Because from Ioannina they took to Thessaloniki. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In that kind of prison, the Germans, they hit him so much, they make him black and blue. We took him into the jail. Then we put in some kind of an ice-- ice water on top of him. He was almost dying. Because he gave away 24 of them. And he hold one. He said, he was holding the one just for souvenir. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, they took us-- from there, they took us to the trains. Soon as we went to the trains over there, we had some packages of food from Red Cross with-- the Germans were taking the most. Whatever they could get it, they took it. It was one pack maybe for five, six people. It was a piece of chocolate or something like that. And we were over there like sardines

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: --on those wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Where was the rest of your family at this time, your mother, your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're together, together. At that time, getting-- getting out from this prison Haidari prison, I told my mother and Dario's family to wait the last commune. Commune is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the car? The carriage of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. The cars they had, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: The wagons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the wagons of the train. The automobiles, the big automobiles, what they called, communes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Truck. They have those kind of trucks. And I told them not to go the first one, to go the-- at the end. Maybe from the end, I say, maybe we're going to jump. Which, our family didn't want that, especially Dario's family, father. Because he said, maybe they're going to kill us. But they don't know how many prisoners they had. So they couldn't stand it on this. And we took not the last one. Even if they-- they could do better than even the first one, to go faster. Anyway, they put us in that trains, in the wagons like sardines again. And I had the experience of this why they were putting on-- on the window in Thessaloniki those kind of pointed wires. So what I did, the first thing I did over there, is I close the window from the inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the train start to go, I opened a window-- was no wires on the window. So long as we were in Greece or Yugoslavia, passing Yugoslavia, I said, we're going to jump from the window. Was no way, because Dario's father [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I was almost half way of me out of the window to jump. And Dario's father, his name was Viktor, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he made such a bad-- for us. He didn't let us jump. Because they thought maybe the Germans are going to kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, I gave up. When I passed Yugoslavia, then I gave up. This way they took right away to Auschwitz after three, four days, five days, I don't remember, and stinking right in the wagons. And because everything was making-- can you imagine all the families over there with no bathroom, with no nothing, no water? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people were dying inside, too. And so soon as they took-- took us to Auschwitz, it was almost night time I guess. They didn't let us have any water, the German. Because they said the water was contaminated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took care of us not to drink water, not to die. And on the other side, they took us at that time all the clothes. They gave us a shower, a hot shower. Because they were afraid, too, not to get an illness from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you get to that point, tell us what happened when you arrived. From the minute you arrived and they opened the trains, what do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We came down. Everybody's thirsty, hungry. And the family's crying, what are they doing to us and so on and so on. They didn't what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as soon as we-- we got in Auschwitz, they were over there. The officer, like everybody knows now, officer pointing with a finger left or right to take the children with the mothers on one side. Older-- older people on the other side. And the young people to go to work on the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know about those things. And right away, of course, children and mothers and everybody else, they took them right to the crematoriums, there, where I was working after that, where they put me to work over there. So, and the young people, they took us apart. And they put-- the next day, they-- they got us to Birkenau in quarantine, maybe day, couple days or three, I don't know, from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, before this when you were separated from your mother, where you were able to say anything to each other before they left, taked to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, Carol. I get emotional. I don't want to say nothing about it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] [SOB] [SOBBING] I love my mother too much. But I never showed to her, never. I never showed to her how much I loved her. And I remember like it is this minute. They are in front of me. We are in one line over here, the young people. The other people, the oldest, with the mothers and the kids. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know how I left the line of mine. I could get a shot from the German, because I went out of my line. And I ran to my mother. [SOB] And I kiss her. [SOBBING] For the first time in my life, I kissed her. Or, the last kiss. The first and the last kiss I gave to her. [SOBBING] And I never saw her again. That's all. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I never saw her again. So they took us where they took us. The-- in the shower like I told you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My brother, he start to cut the hair with the machine, you know, zero. They cut off the hair. And he got a piece of bread. I don't know from where, from whom. He got a big [INAUDIBLE]. Because he was cutting the hair of the [INAUDIBLE]. And he saw me. And he threw me a piece of bread. We were-- we were hungry. When we come out from the shower, and we no hair anymore. We no have clothes with a kind of wooden shoes they give us and so on. We was looking to each other. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nobody recognize-- I did not recognize my brother. My brother didn't recognize me, because were-- we were so much different. Anyway, after that, we were-- they put us the numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your-- they tattooed your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh?

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave you a tattoo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was a couple of guys-- guys over there and [INAUDIBLE], bah, bah, bah, bah, bah, bah, bah. And they put on us the numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show us your number, please, and tell us what it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [SOBBING] number they gave me [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember the most in German, this number. Because in there, I have to think about what number I got. In Germany, we were numbers only, not human beings. My number over there, I was-- always when they were calling me. They-- if you didn't know your number when they call you, and to say, I'm present, then I got shot. So I remember this only German, which [NON-ENGLISH]. That's all what I remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Dario had one number more than I am or less, my brother, too, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after that, they took us to Birkenau, Birkenau, which was 4 kilometers out of Auschwitz. In the mean time, Auschwitz [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was not barracks, wooden barracks. Auschwitz was a building with stones and bricks and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was looking beautiful. Everybody was going out in the morning to work with the music [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at the door and coming back with the music. And over there was a sign saying, [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember that. In the meantime, they were going maybe 1,000 people out to work, was going back maybe 200, 300 less. They would get killed at work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after that, we learned that in Auschwitz, they had a crematoriums, yes. But was for few people dying over there, 100 people, 200 people, 300 people, didn't mean nothing. It was in the thousands-- killing in the small crematoriums. In Auschwitz was the mirror of the concentration camp, was the mirror. Because everything clean. They even had over there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a-- in some rooms, they even prostitutes. But the prostitutes just for the German criminals. They were kapos. They were going to those prostitutes. And, of course, they had better food. And if somebody-- somebody was going was-- it was not so easy to go in there, maybe the Red Cross and so on. What they were showing was showing Auschwitz, beautiful. They're not suffering over here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you see these prostitutes? Did you ever seen one go there or know anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No way. No way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were not showing the-- the prisoners. Maybe-- maybe they were showing the music, going out and in. They were showing whatever they wanted to look better there, maybe the kitchens or the food that we're eating, which was not [INAUDIBLE]. The-- the better food was for the criminals.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you know about prostitutes? And what was-- how did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because over there, were too many friends in Auschwitz, was even my brother-in-law. And they told us about all those things, which I didn't see it. Because I was not there. I was in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took him back [INAUDIBLE] in Birkenau. And they put us in line and asking to everybody-- a German over there with a kapo. The kapo usually was a German criminal or Polands, which were there maybe two, one, two years. They know all about it. They-- what was the idea of the Germans? What was that [INAUDIBLE] idea, to be somebody else. Do it better and to be stronger. And the other ones, the other prisoners, they didn't have much to do. They were all so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] deb-- what do you call them-- Debilitated. Skinny?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no power. I mean, those kapos, they were too strong about it. They were hitting us whenever they wanted. So this was the idea of the Germans to make somebody else stronger than the other. So, and be stronger over the other, he didn't care about if you're Jewish or not Jewish, just to save himself. That was the tricks of the Germans doing all, like this, like they did in Baron Hirsch with this Hason. Hason was an organ of them. Because Hason was taking all the-- the gold and everything and giving to the Germans, a bunch of thieves. That's all they were-- they were doing, the Germans, just stealing from the Jewish people whatever they could. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the end, I forgot to tell you. Those two gold coins, where are they before? When we were in Auschwitz, they left all the-- the clothes. We swallowed again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we went to Birkenau, we got them out again. And I'll tell you what happened to those gold coins. When in Birkenau, they put us in line, where ever, they were asking us what kind of a job was. They say, who's a mechanic? Who's a plumber? Who's a-- so on. And somebody was lifting their hand. And I'm so, I'm so, I'm so, I'm so, I'm so. So Dario was telling me what we were going to say? So I thought saying that we are barbers. Because we needed there to be barbers. They were cutting their hairs all over. And the barber always got something, bits of bread, or something else for somebody else. Dario told me, I'm not a barber. I don't know nothing about it. Don't worry, you're going to be with me, I told him. So, who's a barber? I don't remember. They say in German. Who's a barber? I said, me. Dario, too. OK, Come with me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this kapo, Poland kapo, Jewish, he took us to a kind of a room somewhere. I don't remember where was that, near to the crematoriums, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no idea about the crematoriums. We didn't even know that crematoriums exist over there. Because in Auschwitz after the bath and so on, we had a chance to talk with some Poland-- Poland's guys that were working over there. And they was telling-- was telling us all the Jewish phshh.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember that. They-- they were showing us, phshh. You see that? You see that? It was a couple chimney somewhere, 4 kilometers turn away from us. We could see it, the smoke.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Jewish, phsh, crematoriums. And we said, it's maybe some kind of a meat-- [INAUDIBLE] meat and so on. But even that we-- we could not believe it that the Germans were killing us en masse. It was impossible. No human brain could understand or believe that the Germans or some other human people-- if they were human people, because they're animals to us-- would do such a thing. Well, we didn't know what to say at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were telling to those Polands, [NON-ENGLISH]. They were looking at us, [NON-ENGLISH]? This is the [NON-ENGLISH], the muscles. At the other time, we saw from Birkenau, we saw the gypsies come.

speaker: interviewee
text: The gypsies come with over there women. We saw children. We saw men. And we learned that over there [INAUDIBLE] they own cantina buying with coupons some food from that. And we were saying that there's the families. Our families even got burned already. And we thought that still living, because we saw these families over there. Anyway, this kapo in Birkenau after we said we were barber with Dario, they took us to a big room over there. My god. When opened the doors, what we saw over there sardines just with their head down, all women dead bodies. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how were in there, 500, 600, 1,000. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the kapo took us, took one of the scissor. They gave us two big scissors like that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They want to use it for the-- for lambs, to cut the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for the sheeps, they cut the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The wool. They cut the wool from--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the wool, to cut the wool so big. So he took one of the scissor. He went on top of those dead bodies, get into a women's hair like that, and cut it, and through their hair's down. [INAUDIBLE] cut them [INAUDIBLE], threw them down. This is the way you're going to do. We got so scared, we, Dario. Because even when we were young, talking about dead people or about cemeteries, we were scared at that time. Because were some scaries of-- the scary stories, they were-- and we were scare about dead bodies, dead people, so on. When we saw that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are the-- are the people dead over here? And so, but we were scared. Because the kapo had a stick on his hand and were beating us. So we went on top of the-- when we were over there, we start-- he got again the scissor. And he said, faster, faster.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was going, jumping from one body to another, to another, [INAUDIBLE] this kapo. Bah, bah, bah, bah. With Dario, we could not do that. We were going to get the hair from the women just little by little. Because we were scared, and cut little by little. And all this hair was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was so dirty. from the women. They vomit, you know, from the gas and so on. So after that, we started to do faster, faster. We [INAUDIBLE]. Start [INAUDIBLE] do it faster. He was looking at me. I was looking on him, at Dario. At one moment, he went, Dario, toward the top over there. Because they were one woman to another. And we couldn't get their heads do easy. So he went to on top. There was a woman over there standing like this. And Dario, I guess, he stepped on the stomach of this woman. And-- and one moment, that woman, I think, she got out some gas of her stomach and made a ooh, something like that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saw Dario jumping from the top of the thing going out of the door. He got so scared. And then after that in quarantine we were. They were taking us to work here and there. And then they-- they put us-- to put them in the crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that part--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like you please to tell us about the quarantine. And tell us, who was the kapo? Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you used to call him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We-- we didn't call him. We didn't have to call them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know his name ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew about it when I was working the crematorium, I know the kapos names.

speaker: interviewer
text: But a kapo who shower you how to cut the hair, the one who got you started? The Polish. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Were almost Polands-- he was alive over there for a year, two years, three years, working over there Are on those things. The only kapo I remember, he was in the crematoriums when I was working on the crematorium [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two. And then they took us to crematorium one. One kapo, a German kapo, which they put him over there instead to him in jail, a criminal-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] his name was Carlo. Carlo.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop right now. We're going to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the-- That's the-- only kind of kapos I remember the names. Maybe Dario remembers better than I do.
